items.headline: Five-home development approved on Sherly Place and Lincoln
items.summary: A new residential development called Sherly Place is adding five single-family homes in the $447k-$570k range across multiple lots on Sherly Place and Lincoln Avenue. This represents meaningful new housing supply in San José's tight market.

items.headline: City Council reviews FY 2026-27 capital improvement projects
items.summary: Mountain View City Council held a study session on April 14 to examine the Capital Improvement Program for fiscal year 2026-27, which outlines planned infrastructure and development investments. This is a key opportunity to understand what physical projects and improvements the city is prioritizing for the coming year.

items.headline: City Council Introduces Changes to Tenant Protection Rules
items.summary: Sunnyvale is amending its Residential Tenant Protections Program ordinance, potentially affecting renter rights and landlord obligations in the city. The specific changes were not detailed in the agenda, but this signals a policy shift on tenant-landlord matters worth monitoring.

items.headline: City releases Children and Youth Services Master Plan status
items.summary: The City Council received an update on its Children and Youth Services Master Plan, along with annual reports on youth programs including the Safe Summer Initiative and youth intervention services. This gives residents insight into how the city is planning and funding support for young people.

items.headline: Library Facilities and Customer Experience Plan moves forward
items.summary: The council reviewed the status of San José's Library Facilities and Customer Experience Plan on April 9th. This plan will shape future improvements to library buildings and services that residents use.

items.headline: Downtown Building Gets Historic Upgrade and Extra Floor Space
items.summary: The City Council is considering elevating 405 Kipling Street's historic designation from Category 4 to Category 2, which would allow the building to add 5,000 square feet of floor area as an incentive for seismic and historic rehabilitation. This allows a developer to build more while preserving the building's historic character.
